A simple way to make the job hunt a little more enjoyable | Hi all! I, like many of you, am suffering through the slog of trying to find a job. This entails sitting in various cafes 6 hours a day everyday applying to as many jobs as I can (and are relevant) until my head feels like it’ll explode if I have to write/edit one more cover letter. It also means constant prep for phone screenings, cleaning my room for video conference calls with potential employers, and trekking around the city going for interviews. Its strangely exhausting and stressful yet also often boring. 

Something I’ve recently been doing to infuse a little fun into the process is exploring the area where my interview will be beforehand. I take the train over to where my interview is and it’s usually a new part of the city, so I’ve started to get there an hour or two early and walk around a bit, explore, go into shops or just see what the area has for me to check out. It makes me feel a lot better to have a little fun and I walk into my interview feeling a little lighter. 

If you find that you have the time to do this, I highly recommend it!

The new company is a software/mobile app company and I will likely be starting in the SQA side of things, and moving to engineering when I get more familiar with their software and workflow. Applied for grad SE role. Got bumped up | As the title says, I applied for a grad role at a large tech company, maybe not quite Big N but very large none the less. Did some aptitude/mental agility tests and then had a very casual in person interview, no coding / whiteboard tests involved. 

Few days later I check the status of my application on their jobs portal and see a new second application listed for "software engineer" as being at the interview stage, with the grad role now no longer under consideration.

Now, I'm not a 21 year old fresh out of college looking for my first job. I'm mid 30s, just completed part time degree, and have worked in a technology and management role for about 6 years. But the fact remains, I have never worked as a SE and I will be starting from scratch!! But sure enough, I get an offer email, and instead of it being a fixed term grad contract with grad salary, it's a full time permanent SE contract with SE salary.

Naturally, I am over the moon. But also a little terrified that I've oversold myself, especially considering there was zero coding involved in the interview process! I think imposter syndrome is upon me, and I haven't even started my first day!

Anyone have this kind of experience?

A Tiny Guide To "Grinding" Leetcode Problems | Hey all,

​

I'm still pretty young and only in my 2nd year of college so I often can't really contribute to this subreddit in terms of raw experience...but I wanted to make a post for those of you who are studying "Leetcode" type problems to pass interviews at Big N companies.

​

I have done about 250 Leetcode problems in all problem categories and read the fantastic book "Elements of Programming Interviews" by Adnan Aziz, Tsung-Hsien Lee, and Amit Prakash about 5 times and skimmed it 3 times.

​

Really really really recommend EPI over CTCI but that is personal preference. EPI dives very very deep into the reasoning behind how we go from brute force solutions for each problem, to the optimal solution but it is less beginner friendly since it is more intellectually rigorous. It instills an apparatus of thinking that no other resource I have found does.

​

I want to make a small guide answering questions people often have about these problems as well as things to watch out for.

# An Exhaustive List of Topics You'll Need To Know Well

* **Fundamentals of Computers** (just a general knowing how computers store information etc.)
   * This is just a basal thing. Knowing how binary works, how memory is managed in a program (stack & heap), etc.
* **Big O Time & Space Complexity Computation**
   * Know asymptotic bounds. If you can be flexible in how you analyze a solution you come up with (lower bounding it, upper bounding, exact bounding) it can help you see whether you can do better and make an improved algorithm
* **Arrays**
   * This is pretty straightforward. Often questions that work within arrays will be solved in linear time ( O(n) ) for the most part and that linear time solution will be tricky.
* **Primitives**
   * Things like bit shifting. This is more rare and I don't think this is as important since it doesn't test real thinking abilities since it mixes with one's abilities to bit shift which is an esoteric skill.
* **Strings**
   * These are problems that often deal with strings like permutations, backtracking problems that have use take an exhaustive approach in producing decompositions of a string to search a possibility space (which is often a brute force way of solving a problem since it will be exponential in time), etc etc. String problems are often solved most optimally in O(n) time or O(s1 + s2) time (linear with respect to each string) if we are given 2 strings...whatever the problem may be.
* **Dynamic Programming**
   * One of the most difficult subjects. This is the key: subproblems. If you can identify the subproblem, you have cracked the problem. Because from there it is all about memoization to cache and leverage previous solutions.
* **Recursion / Backtracking**
   * This is a comfort thing. The more of these you do, the better you get. At some point you will naturally think of solutions in a recursive manner (if backtracking could be a possible approach used). Problems that use backtracking often say..."generate all"..."compute every"....this indicates an expression of exhaustively expressing all the possibilities of a decision space. Recursion is beautiful for this.
* **Graphs**
   * Know DFS & BFS. DFS uses a stack (either implicit with the call stack and recursion or explicit if we create our own stack) & BFS uses a queue.
* **Greedy Algorithms**
   * These are algorithms that take the locally most optimal solution to achieve a global optimal. In contrast to problems that use dynamic programming (which is characterized by caching previous subproblems to find a global optimum), greedy algorithms take locally optimal choices.
   * Not all greedy approaches one comes up with will work 100% of the time so it hinges on being able to use deductive logic to prove that a given approach will always work
* **Hashtables**
   * Very very very common in mid-level interviews. This is a must know. It is pretty simple, when our time complexity is too high, we can often reduce time and increase space by using some sort of auxiliary structure to cache work. Hashtables are often that auxiliary structure.
* **Linked Lists**
   * A tricky structure to work with. It is hard because we can't index into items. It gets easier with time but always remain tricky.
* **Sorting**
   * Know the fundamental sorting algorithms. Bubble, Insertion, Selection, Merge, Quick, Heap, ...etc
* **Searching**
   * If an array is sorted. IMMEDIATELY know that that is a strong hint that the optimal solution will use binary search and stay to the order of O(log(n))
* **Min/Max Heaps**
   * Heaps are really cool. Just know...if you see "find the LARGEST"...or ... "find the SMALLEST"...anything to do with size...think heap. Min or max. If we want larger items we use a min heap since we can throw away small items (to leave the large ones behind). And vice versa for when we want the smaller items
* **Stacks**
   * LIFO structures. Know how to implement a stack inside out. it is fairly easy so don't fear it. I'd suggest knowing how to implement all data structures stated here. Why not?
* **Queues**
   * FIFO structures. Used for Breadth First Search.
* **Trees, Binary Trees, & Binary Search Trees**
   * Trees are connected, acyclic, graphs. You can do DFS and BFS on them. Print all the characters in the tree in this order? Does it look like DFS? You can do that. Traverse the tree level by level? Looks like BFS. (BFS goes out level by level).
* **System and OO design Principles (sometimes)**
   * Some compaines ask Object Oriented questions. A great great resource for this is the book "Clean Architecture" by Robert C. Martin. I also highly recommend his book "Clean Code" but it won't help you for interviews (but will make you a better programmer)

# An Approach To Preparation

1. Find your weak topics. For me those were trees, backtracking, dynamic programming, and linked lists.
2. Start with easys. My first Leetcode problem was Jewels & Stones (you can search it) and it took me 30 minutes......it was just 2 for loops. Am I dumb? ... maybe ... but these problems are so far divorced from daily programming tasks that it was difficult for me.
3. Easys will be very difficult when you just start...then they will start getting...easy...hmmmm...onto the mediums.
4. Your summer internship interviews will be medium difficulty questions. Full-time roles will be upper medium questions sprinkled with a few hard questions. Stay rooted in the fundamentals above and you can survive.
5. Go onto hards if you want...but don't get lost on esoteric problems that require "special" tricks. This is all about getting ready to pass an interview for a job, not so much to have bragging rights.
6. Top it all off with polishing your delivery. [pramp.com](https://pramp.com) interviews helped me immensely. I did about 8 in person interviews this past season.

# F.A.Q.

**How many Leetcode until I'm ready?**

There is no finite amount. Every person comes to the table with their own weaknesses in all topics above. You will know when you are ready. You will see a problem and say..."Oh...yeah I know what principle to apply here". The more you get that spark, the higher the chances you pass.

​

**Should I time myself?**

Yes and no. Time is critical. Speed is critical. But timing yourself is useless if you are very uncomfortable with a specific problem class. I suggest solving many problems (peeping the solutions often is fine...just gain comfort) in your weak points. Then when you get sick of jumping to answers you will soon take the leap and just solve the problem yourself because you will become familiar with the techniques required for the approach. (this is how backtracking was for me. I went from total confusion to it becoming a default way of thinking.)

​

**What should I focus my studying on?**

Weak points. And then popular problems. Find a list of problems the company you are interviewing at asks. No idea whether this is a myth or not (and CTCI addressed this as false...that companies repeat questions from a list) but I have friends that told me of getting exactly questions from these lists. It isn't critical but it can help.

​

**Reading books vs. Leetcode/HackerRank?**

Books give you theory. Coding gives you the memory in your fingers and the necessary practice. Like...if I know a problem will use BFS, how fast can I put the logic in place for a basic search? If I know that a problem may use a heap...how fast can I throw up a priority queue with the right comparator (if it is a max or min heap...Java defaults to a min heap without a comparator)

​

Hope this helps someone...or made any sense...I typed it up quickly.

​

I'm honestly not THAT good...like problems still stump me...but I've come a long way so my experiences may help someone else.

​

I'll make more posts if people want more small tips.

​

Got caught job hunting by my boss. Decided to be honest and everything turned out well. | Long story short, my boss found out I was job hunting today by accident but it was in a way that I could have plausibly denied it. 

I thought about lying to my boss and making up excuses about how it wasn't related to job seeking. After reading multiple threads here about getting caught job seeking, it seemed like the general consensus in these situations was to deny, deny, deny.

But after all the support and positive vibes my boss had given me, I couldn't do it. Instead, I set aside time and came clean about my motivations, rationale, and timeframe for leaving. My boss said he knew, but was very happy I was honest because it meant he could plan for the future more concretely. He gave me his full support and asked me when I wanted to leave.

Morale of the story: not all companies are out to screw you. If you have a good relationship with your boss and your company, don't burn bridges.

One day update: Still in the clear! Everything has returned to normal so far, but we did have a discussion on what I wanted to work on before I left.

I finally did it! Unemployed for a year and took a risk that will pay off | I've never posted here before but I want to share my story and hope it might help inspire some of you who went through similar situations or are still in the job hunt.

A little bit of background about me. I graduated in May 2018 with a Bachelors in Economics. During my undergraduate years, I didn't go out much nor did I really pursue any real opportunities. I did not hold a single job but was involved in some of the student clubs so I had that going for me. I spent a lot of my years in my room playing video games (I still do) and really avoided anything that could land me an internship because I was afraid of rejection. As a result, I didn't have a resume up until my junior year and at that point, I still was reluctant to look for things. Time went by fast and before I knew it, I was a senior with no internship experience and literally zero job experience in my life. I did have some activities though such as some of the clubs I was in but that was it. Alongside that, my GPA wasn't exactly the best, higher than a 3.0 but not by much.

After graduation, I started applying for jobs. I knew I wanted a job in the financial services or tech industry. Initially, I was a bit delusional and thought I could land a solid job but my expectations quickly faded as weeks went on without a single response back. After a month of applying endlessly, I realized that the job hunt process was tiring and brutal but I pushed on. I used LinkedIn, Indeed, and all sorts of different job boards to apply and look for jobs. I tried to get out at least 10 applications a day before I went back to playing video games and this went on until September of that year where I would get my first interview. Because I had no interview skills, I ended up not making it past the first round. Over the course of next few months, this cycle would repeat where I would send out many applications and maybe 1 would get back to me. Sometimes even after getting back to me, I would get ghosted or not do well on the interviews. This went on for sometime and I started feeling helpless as I had to start paying my loans and did not have the means to do so. The only positive side of all of this was that I was living at home and all the jobs I was applying for was in my city (NYC). Instead of applying for some retail jobs to make some money, I was determined to land something I wanted and kept sending applications out mindlessly.

By this point, roughly 7 months had passed by and I had several interviews but couldn't convert any to an offer. I did start to get pretty good at them but I still didn't know why I wasn't getting anything. I was getting desperate and embarrassed and I eventually looked into my school's one-year MBA program. I was worried because I had loans and my family was in a bad financial situation. However, my parents were very encouraging and after consulting a lot of my friends and family, I decided to pursue it. I saw it as a second chance to take all the opportunities I had missed as an undergrad but I knew I would have to really try for it.

I'm currently a few months in and I graduate from my program in May 2020. I have attended many different career events and have done a lot compared to what I had done as an undergrad. I finally have received an offer a few days ago and it feels like a huge boulder has been lifted off my shoulders. The offer exceeded my expectations and I couldn't be more happy with it.

I wanted to share this story because I know how brutal the whole job application can be. The last year had been hell for me but it did teach me a lot about the whole process. In the end, it really can be a numbers game and luck does play a factor. For those who are still out there looking, I want you to know that you're not alone. There will be always be up and downs and it takes a lot to push forward but at the end of the day, you only need to land one job. I know my story isn't unique but I know that some of you can relate and push forward. 

If there's anything you guys want to ask, feel free to shoot me a pm.

Desperately want out of IT, where to go? | Hello, person in my mid 30's here having a somewhat premature midlife crisis of sorts. I got out of school at 20 with an AA degree, then proceeded to spend (waste) the rest of my 20's going from random job to random job with no direction - cashier, pizza delivery, server, factory work, etc. Eventually, I took up a call center position for a few months that turned into a help desk spot that had me thinking "hey maybe IT is for me". Fast forward a couple years into a different help desk position that has me thinking "hey IT is definitely not for me". 

For the past couple of months I've been trying to study to get certifications to become a sys admin, and it is an absolute slog. I feel like I am getting nowhere, forcing myself to study for something I have next to no interest in. I don't lack motivation, I have no problem coming home from work and working on projects and learning things I'm actually interested in, things more on the creative side - video editing, music, mixing/audio engineering, Illustrator/Photoshop/AE (I have a lot of hobbies, I am good at none of them). I've been into design since a teen (have actually done some freelance illustration work), so my instinct would say I should go into graphic design, but from my research that seems like a hard field to get into, and if I ever did my starting salary would be much less than what I am making now. I have also looked into UI/UX design, but from looking at listings for UI/UX jobs in my area the requirements seem so extensive I frankly feel like I am too dumb to ever get enough knowledge where I could get one of those jobs. 

I just wish I had a clear cut path I could follow or a definitive goal in mind to work towards so I could feel not so aimless, but I have no idea what to do or how to get to the point of even choosing, there's so many possibilities and so much to learn and know, it's daunting! What is out there for me?!

Just got a job offer!!! | I was laid off at the end of January so I am 3 months in to my job search and I just accepted a job offer for a company that I had worked third party with previously.  I was fortunate enough to have a little padding from severance plus unemployment benefits to carry me through. Since I had the chance, I figured this was the perfect time to jump industries and try for something completely new. I have applied for lots of jobs that have are not even close to my field and have actually had some good response back on my resume from recruiters and interviewers.  I am somewhat terrible at selling myself on paper but  I love to interview, (seriously, I could be a serial interviewee). I tend not to get emotionally invested in a job or company unless I am really excited about the actual job responsibilities.  Two weeks ago I was certain I had interviewed and was on track to get the perfect job then I was rejected. That was a really tough ego blow because I had let myself identify with the job before it was even mine and when it didn't happen I questioned everything about my job experience, finding a job outside of my industry, etc...

There have been so many ups and downs and soooooo much ghosting from companies. I came across another redditor's post about their process and it helped me stay grounded and positive about what it really looks like for a job seeker. So, I decided to share my stats on what it took for me to finally land my job offer:

 From  January to April

Registered with one employment agency but didn't feel as though that was a productive move as it never generated a lead and their competency testing system was extremely outdated so I scored very low in areas (excel, office, power point) that I know I excel in.

Daily searches of Indeed, Linkedin, Zip-recruiter, Glassdoor, and my state's workforce website 

Over 100 application/resume submissions

4 reworks of my resume-some to target specific industries others just to fine tune it

9 face to face interviews

12 preliminary phone interviews/screenings

1 Skype interview

1 offer rejected-company culture was not compatible with my beliefs

4 actual email/phone rejections following interviews

3 interviews with no feedback offered, no offer or rejection

1 job offer accepted

​

Whatever happens stay positive, patient, and persistent.  I hope this helps in someway.

I got a job offer after 3 months of unemployment!! | Hey y'all!

So I graduated in May with a degree in American Studies ("the most unemployable major," according to my no-good college counselor, who tried to use that as a reason to consider law school, \[*like I need more student debt*\]).  I had a pretty prestigious internship  for two years of college that unfortunately didn't lead to a full time opportunity. 

I applied to 392 jobs this summer. I participated in 26 phone screenings and 10 in person interviews. There were 3 jobs that related to my experience and long term goal. I advanced to the last round in each, where I among the top 3-5 candidates, getting my hopes up for each. Two of them rejected me. The last one I hadn't heard from in 3 weeks, and couldn't get a response to my follow up emails. 

I totally understand how easy it is to fall into depression during periods of unemployment and job searching. I couldn't even get a job in a restaurant or retail. It truly sucked seeing my friends starting their first jobs post-graduation, grabbing happy hour drinks after work, and sharing work stories, while I just felt stagnant and incompetent. LinkedIn became a toxic place, because every new job update was proof that everybody was more successful than me. I drank more. I smoked more. I found it hard to get out of bed-- even harder to leave the house. Anxiety kept me from sleeping through the night. I avoided friends and family, because I was ashamed of myself for still being unemployed. I burned through my savings, realizing that I had about a month until I had to reach out to my family for support which they couldn't provide.

But then the third job got back to me. It is exactly what I want to do with my long term career goals. It is also at a company that is ranked #1 in its field, and shares my values. They are paying me $15,000/year more that I asked for. My future supervisors are people I already look up to from our conversations in the interview. It's not going to be an easy job - the company has a reputation for expecting the best and nothing less - but I am passionate about what I will be doing, and up for the challenge.  

I want to thank the people on this board for their advice, tips, and stories. It really helped me and guided me in what has been the most difficult and painful period of my young life. I used to roll my eyes at the people on here telling struggling unemployed job seekers that everything will work out, and the right opportunity will come when its meant to. Now that it has, I understand. It's hard to see the big picture when you are drowning in your own self pity. 

I start on Tuesday :)

Yet another entry level job search stat | Sharing my very recent job search stats as a relatively entry level SWE, keep in mind as technically companies no longer considered me as a fresh grad, the numbers might be a bit higher than most of the fresh grad postings on this sub
  
 
  

Location: San Francisco Bay Area  
  
Professional experience: 1 - 1.5 years, first job switch  
  
Time: 3 - 4 weeks from application to offer  
  
Background: multiple internships, 7 projects, Bachelor of Computer Science from University of Waterloo (Canada)  
  
TC: 160 - 180k, non Big N
  
 
  

The numbers:
  
**Applications** - 311

**Phone interview** requests from HR: 42 accepted, 33 declined  
  
* 18 from 3rd party recruiter spams that refuses to disclose the company names, salary band...
  
* 15 reached out after an offer has been made, declined because I'm doubtful they could match the offer
  
**Technical screens**: 25 accepted, 9 declined: take home projects, role isn't a good fit after the introductory call, sketchy company, unreasonable hiring process, timeline won't work out...
  

* 18 coding phone interviews  
* 7 hacker rank or codility coding challenges  
  

**Onsites**: 8 accepted, 2 declined
  
  
 
  

Main points:

- Due to the massive amount of interviews it was not uncommon to be juggling ~4 interviews/day, on week #1 I had 22 interviews scheduled, with some days having up to 7 interviews (3 tech phone screen + 4 recruiter introductory calls)
  
- My working hours was extended to 8am - 10pm during weekday, and 10am - 11pm during weekends
  
- Facebook was the only company out of the 5 Big Ns (Facebook, Microsoft, Apple, Google, Amazon) that did not request a phone interview  
  
- I skipped leetcode-easys and only focused on leetcode-mediums and leetcode-hard, I made it a rule to do at least 2 leetcode-mediums/day (including weekends)
  
 
  

Difference vs. job hunting as a fresh grad: 

- I was initially expecting perhaps 5 - 10% callback ratio given what I've read from this sub, the callback ratio greatly exceeded my expectations, but at the meantime unlike a fresh grad I just don't have unlimited time anymore
- I have a feeling that the complaints on leetcode vs. take-home on this sub might have at least partially contributed to the latest hiring trend: I was pleasantly surprised by how few companies asked take-home projects.
Less than 2 years ago I think the ratio is roughly 20% so you might see 1 take-home out of every 5 company, I think this time only 2 or 3 companies wanted me to do a take-home
- System design rounds are now expected, when I was a fresh grad I don't recall there being any system design questions during onsite, nowdays I think there was at least 1 system design rounds in every onsite that I attended
- Laptop rounds are a new trend: you will bring your own laptop to onsite interviews or they can provide you with one, interviewer will give you the question on the spot, you have 60-90 min (15min intro, 60min coding, 15min presentation)
 just like a technical coding interview but you're free to look up anything you need on the internet, the question could be anything from leetcode, data structure and OOP design, concurrency,

9 hours programming test | Not so long ago I received a mail from some software company, located in Berlin, Germany. Something like "we're looking for C++ developers of all sorts, both senior and junior, since we have a lot of tasks" and yada-yada. Salary is 80-120k euros per year, relocation to Berlin and all that. C++ is not my main language, but I had some decent experience with it in the past, so I decided to send them a resume, because why not.

Soon after I received an invitation to pass an online programming test, which looked like [this](https://i.imgur.com/9H46lWZ.png).

" *The solution has to be submitted within* ***a 9 hour time frame****. Excellent C++ specialists will of course solve the problem in 3 to 4 hours, and we leave it to the candidates to test their solutions thoroughly before handing them in (in case they finish early).* "

Some of you might be even familiar with this form, because, apparently, these guys have been sending those invitations for YEARS (4 is the least, as far as the google search goes). There were even a couple of threads in this subreddit about them in the past. Most popular responses were *"Guys just want a job done for free with this fake test"* and *"9 hours with NDA not worth it"*. After extensive googling I managed to find the test itself. Some really tricky data structure coding. Despite the NDA agreement, people still post it and ask solutions on stackoverflow (who would have thought, wow). I even found multiple mentions of the same task: first mention was dated 2016 and the last was January 2020. Which kinda proves the point, that the test is legit and they don't get "the work done for free". **It's been EXACTLY the same test FOR YEARS.** 

After a certain amount of thinking, I didn't attempt to take the test anyway. There are a couple of working solutions to it on stackoverflow, so I could just copy and paste them. Or I could come up with my own solution (though, I'm not sure if I'd managed in 9 hours with proper testing and bug fixing). And it's not that I just didn't wanna bother. But instead something in this whole thing felt fishy.

So, yesterday I've got a mail labeled as *"Reminder: Your company-name programming test".* Which contained something like *"we noticed you didn't attempt our test. WE BEG YOU, PLEASE DO. It's hard, but very-very rewarding in the end. Working with us is super-cool, you'll love it."* And it also contained the following sentence: *"In fact, of our now* ***20 full-time developers****, in* ***15 years*** *of company-name,* ***only one*** ***has ever quit*** *their job".*

Now THIS made me freak like hell. I initially assumed that this is just some semi-large startup company with may be 100 engineers and may be 5 people hired yearly. That would explain a very demanding test and the fact, that they've been doing this since 2016 (at least).

But 20 developers??! Only **ONE ever** quit their job in 15 years? What the actual fuck? Are they really looking for **ONE DUDE** for 4 years??! What kind of dude would that be? God walking amongst mere mortals? Dennis Ritchie spiritual reincarnation?! And if the dude they are looking for has to be THAT good - why would he go with them instead of Google?

​

P.S.

Sorry for the long post.

For a bit of context, my first job out of college (graduated May 2019) as a web developer only lasted around 6 months. I took a break for one month (for mental health reasons & since Dec is pretty dead in terms of hiring) and started applying at the start of Jan this year. 

For the most part things were going alright, I was getting interviews (which was a pleasant surprise because I thought my short stint would be a major turn off) & in some cases went through several steps that led me to believe that at the bare minimum there was some interest in me. 

Ultimately I was often told that I was a good candidate but of course there was always someone better. So I took this whole process as a numbers game (and believed that with enough applications someone will eventually give me a chance):

- 93 applied 
- 54 ghosted (includes profile views, or status being changed to "under review" but haven't heard back since)
- 25 rejected
- 17 interviews (phone screens, video interviews, in-person interviews, coding tests)
- 9 I withdrew from (some red flags, commute, or turned out to be night shift)
- 5 pending (ghosted in the middle of the process or hiring freeze)
- 1 offer I rejected because of red flags

Fast forward to late Feb-Early March and the pandemic hits hard and suddenly everything is on lockdown. All the companies I was in the middle of interviewing with was hit with a hiring freeze (which is understandable). 

Now I know the IT industry is still pretty intact given that most companies are going virtual and online now, but after going through this rush of balancing phone screens, in-person interviews, coding challenges, & suddenly being met with everything slowing down due to the pandemic (if not stopping altogether), I can't help but feel demotivated.

Lately I can't being myself to continue applying because with everything that's going on, I don't have the mental energy to go on a video interview or answer a coding test that would easily take hours of my time. 

I also can't help but feel like the few applications that I do manage to send end up lost in a void meanwhile the gap on my resume is only going gettingt bigger :(

CodeSignal Tips from someone with 844 & 843 | It seems there's some general anguish about CodeSignal so I thought I may share some tips

I have verified scores of [844](https://imgur.com/6bh0luN) and [843](https://imgur.com/zAVdeXL) spaced a year apart and the most recent one was 3 days ago

I lost points on speed exclusively

You'll notice one question is repeated, but I think it's safe to say I don't remember the soln to a q I did a year ago.

​

Tips:

1. Run often, submit often. You don't lose points for submitting many incorrect solns before a correct one, so don't wait. As soon as you have a soln run and submit it.
2. Do one problem at a time. The way the speed score is determined doesn't help you if you choose to jump between problems before solving one since your total time for the problem is aggregated.
3. Don't format your code. After doing a couple of these I'm almost certain that the formatting or cleanliness of your code does not factor into your final score so don't waste time on it like I did.
4. Don't use a verbose language even if it's fast. 70 min isn't a long time, if you're using Java you're shooting yourself in the foot. Use Python or smth similar so you can write up the solns quickly.
5. If CodeSignal won't print your error use a try except (or equivalent) to print your error
6. Practice working with 2D arrays and maps (dicts). Work on iterating through a 2D array in unconventional ways (i.e. column then row, a spiral inwards, a spiral outwards, skipping certain rows, etc) so you get practice.
7. Implement the easy obvious soln first, don't try to optimize until you hit a TLE. Optimized solns can take more time and don't necessarily get you more points.
8. The data structure they give you isn't the data structure you have to work with. Change the data structure of the input variables if it makes your implementation easier/faster. 
9. Prior to taking the CodeSignal practice one topic at a time. Don't jump from DP to Graph Theory to string manipulation. Take the time to do a set of problems in a specific problem space before moving on. That way you truly start recognizing when a problem fits that pattern.

I'd love to hear if anyone disagrees or has any tips to add.

I recognize t's not advantageous for me to share these tips since I'm also competing for the same positions, but I think some great engineers may lose out on positions simply because of the way CodeSignal works and that would suck for everyone.

After 2.5 years and 500+ applications I landed the big one! | Today I signed my offer letter for a role at an upcoming Digital Marketing firm! Everyone knew about the sales firm, for mostly notorious reasons.  I spoke highly of them of course so as not to create any red flags for potential employers.  After the wear and tear from pressure sales I went to work as a Taphouse manager.  The mom and pop shop that employed me had me fill several roles.  We opened up a brewery, survived Covid-19, and I leave them today with the biggest(and only) period of growth since their maiden year.  They made a very enticing offer for me to take over the distribution of our beer.  I had every intention of accepting this offer. It was enough for me to accept my lot in life so in July, after the offer was made, I finally stopped applying for a dream job.  On August 28th things changed. The very last business that I had applied to contacted me.  The job sounded like a perfect fit. The pay was more. The hours less. The employer would actually supply health insurance.  Of course, no offer would be made until I interviewed 8 times and completed 2 separate assessments.  After 2 and a half years I finally found a job in my field, and the feeling could not be sweeter. Don’t grill people about their availability | I had a lady call me up and ask if I was still interested in coming in for an interview for a place that I applied to a few months back and I said yes. We then proceeded to try and find a date and time for me to come in and she asks if I’m available to come in on Monday. I told her that I was working that day but I was available X Y and Z date next week. She asked me what time I work Monday and I told her afternoons for shift relief and closing. She then asked me about Monday morning and I told her that I’m not available in the mornings (I had also made it clear in my cover letter that I was not available in the mornings, that my availability is afternoons and early evenings). She then starts grilling me about why I’m not available. I told her that my husbands business is just getting back up on its feet again now that covid restrictions are lifting in our area and that we are still waiting for one more employee to come back from their leave. so he needs me in the mornings. She then barks at me “Well what are you going to do when you get a full time job?”

“We would adjust my schedule in his store accordingly.” Apparently that wasn’t the “right” answer as I got a very flippant, snarky “fine we will get back to you.”

Honestly, I should have told her that it’s none of her business and left it at that. Instead I told her the truth and she couldn’t handle it. 

For all you young people, don’t ever let anyone try to guilt you or grill you about what your availability is and why it is what it is. If a boss refuses to respect that as early on as the interview process, walk. The job is not worth it and you deserve better. They showed you their true colours right off the bat. I’m turning 31 in the new year and it took me until my mid to late 20’s to figure out that not only do I not deserve that kind of grief, but to actually learn what it’s like to work for a good boss that respects their employees. Once I got a taste of that, I was permanently hooked. 


Never ever allow someone to convince you that you need to light yourself on fire to keep someone else warm.

Most job posts out there are fake - thats why you cant get a job. | Oh there are 20 000 Jobs posted in your Region? Why then cant you get a job after sending out 100+ resumes? Because most of them are fake. The same job is posted on 5 or 10 different websites - and suddenly the 20 000 Jobs posted become 5000 in reality.

But wait - of these remaining  5000 "real" jobs half are fake posts made deliberately by the firm/corporation. And suddenly the supposed 20 000 jobs in your region shrink down to less than 2000.

Why would companies do that?

– Fake job postings exist so that employers can gauge the current  talent pool. The number of  applications can be a valuable pointer on what to pay a person for a  given job.  It also is an indicator of how easy that person is to potentially replace.

– Fake job postings exist so that companies can get a back-up for  your position and keep resumes on file. Since we already know that every  job is temporary, an enterprising employer might be stockpiling talent in case you make a sudden exit, or, are asked to make an exit.

– Fake job postings exist to make it easier for nepotism and other unfair hiring practices to occur. When the boss wants his nephew hired it’s not always cut and dry. In many organizations, the company needs  to “look outside” for suitable candidates. Searching externally for a  candidate can also satisfy any potential Equal Employment Opportunity  (EEO) mandates.  This is a quick and dirty way to appear fair.

You cannot get a job because most job openings do not exist. Sure there are some jobs that are not posted despite beeing avaliable but these are the minority. The sad truth is that most jobs that are posted out there are fake and dont exist. Coupled with the ridiculous requirements companies demand nowadays, thats the reason why people cannot find a job.

We compiled a library of realistic engineering take-home tests and ranked them |  

[https://www.trytapioca.com/library-of-assessments](https://www.trytapioca.com/library-of-assessments)

Studies show that a work sample test is the best predictor of candidate performance on the job, which is why many software engineering teams use take-home tests as one step in their hiring process. But designing an effective test is difficult and time-consuming. For example, candidates are reluctant to complete tests that are too long or not engaging enough. But make them too short and teams won’t get the signal they need for a proper evaluation.

To encourage more thoughtful test design (and hopefully save future candidates from the worst offenders), my team compiled the *largest library of non-“whiteboard” take-home tests* that real engineering teams have used. You’ll find the challenges that Stripe and Microsoft gave to their full-stack candidates, front-end tests from Tailwind and Rivian, and back-end ones from Basecamp and Revolut. Whether you’re looking to evaluate an Android, DevOps, or Data Science candidate, a bootcamp grad, or senior engineer, we found a few options for each.

Having built 20+ tests ourselves, we also rated the design of each test. The criteria for a 5-star rating:

1. Tests for skills highly relevant to those required for the position

2. Includes a well-written description of the prompt and even motivation for using a take-home test

3. Sets clear expectations for candidates (e.g. time requirements, evaluation criteria, submission details)

4. Asks for a reasonable time commitment from candidates (<4 hours)

A few notes: - We found most of these test prompts in public GitHub repos, usually owned by the hiring team but occasionally in the candidate-owned submission. We sifted through hundreds of tests and filtered out those overly focused on algorithms (aka LeetCode), leaving us with 142 tests in the library.

\- The larger and more recognizable companies didn’t always have the best tests. Some of the most interesting prompts we found were from smaller teams (e.g. YC startups). This shouldn’t be surprising. Startups need to design candidate-friendly hiring experiences to compete for talent against more established players.

\- There were common themes among the tests we found. For example, front-end candidates were often given a Figma design + content feed to implement, while back-end candidates had to implement an API given a set of requirements. Data scientists were usually given a data set to clean, analyze, and submit a Jupyter notebook with their findings.

\- We’ll continue to update this library and add descriptions of each test so it’s easier to compare.

Have feedback, or another take-home test we should add? We’d love to hear from you!

No streamlined/automated build process. I write software installation scripts. I install software on payloads. And I verify the software works. That’s the bulk of this job. I get tasked with integrating other vendor’s apps into the aforementioned SDK, except the vendor developed their software with an SDK version that is quite literally years older than the most recent. And it’s my job to integrate despite that the SDK has changed massively since then. 

Since November I’ve been applying and trying to leave. I think my resume is fine, as I consistently get phone/tech interviews for roles that are relevant to what I want to do and did in the past in undergrad (embedded development mainly). I’ve had probably 5-6 promising interviews. 

The problem is I cannot keep up with even technical questions in phone screens, let alone actual technical interviews. The breadth of questions I receive for these interviews, even for 1 year of experience, is just difficult for me to keep up with given the fact that I use absolutely none of it in my current role. I’m absolutely regressing as a software engineer and it’s only getting worse the longer I stay here. 

How do I escape this? It seems like a catch 22. For the roles I’m interested in, I need experience in development whereas my current role only gives me experience in integration. The more “experience” I have, the more I’m expected to know for those roles, except my knowledge only tends to regress. Any advice? I do my best to keep up to speed during my free time but it’s more difficult than I thought it would be.

Job Searching is hard.. HANG IN THERE. | I'm been extremely grateful in my ability to find a job that'll help me kickstart my career despite me not having a single ounce of experience in my current field... I just wanted to share my story to give hope to others. Please note, the numbers I share are about 95% accurate. 

I got laid off in February from a very cozy warehouse position where i worked off the books and got paid daily. My former boss/warehouse owner decided it would be better to rehire the person i had replaced because he was "forced" by his insurance to hire back a dude who deliberately wished to destroy his livelihood instead of keeping a competent and reliable worker... 

Suffering from depression and having been asked several times by my former employer to come back, i rejected his requests because i refused to work in an environment where i wasn't appreciated even though there were less than 5 total employees and I was one of two warehouse associates. 

I gave himself a week break but i jumped right on the search for jobs. I had updated my resume, created two other versions of my resume with different templates, updated my references, and my skills. I added a STUPID AMOUNT of keywords in white text and left it between the visible text to attempt to fool the algorithms. From the second week of February up until the beginning of July, I made it my new job to wake up every morning at 7am, apply to jobs nonstop from 8AM-4PM every single day. Every single fucking day.
For five and a half months, I was so low and depressed. Between the shitty economy, recession, and my overly populated city thanks to these hundreds of thousands of immigrants, I was struggling to find a job in NYC. Within this time span, I used several screens to constantly apply to any and EVERY SINGLE JOB i saw on Indeed, LinkedIn, ZipRecruiter,  TODAY, Fivver, even Craigslist. I sent out between 5-15 jobs every hour. I made it my goal to do a minimum of 40 applications a day. Within these 5.5 months, I sent over 2,300 applications. This number is a very rough estimate based on the amount of mail i received in my email. The *real* number of applications is certainly over 2,000 applications though.. 

I got a total of 6 interviews. Within those 2,000+ applications in 5.5 months. 4 of these interviews were group interviews that wasted my time as they were pyramid schemes. 1 job rejected me after a formal interview. My current job accepted me after several rounds of interviewing. 
Now, three months after my employment, I'm very happy with my first time office job. I'm comfortable with my starting pay, wonderful benefits including health insurance, plenty of PTO, and a set schedule with my weekends free. Constant office snacks, paid meals biweekly, yearly raises, and the company encourages promoting within before they offer jobs to the general public. I plan to use this position to kickstart my career in the medical industry as a stepping stone, working towards becoming a phlebotomist. 

If you've made it this far, thank you for this lengthy read... I only wrote this to continue to encourage others to focus on that grind to better your employment and better your life. Please do not be discouraged. Please do not give up. I felt like giving up after my first thousand applications. I did quit searching (for about a week) after my second thousand applications. I continued to search because I didn't have a choice and I needed to find a job. The reality is, this world is your oyster. Set a goal for yourself and work hard to secure your position in a proper company that cares about you. Life is uncertain, but the only thing you CAN rely on, is yourself. 

TLDR: Took 5 and a half months and over 2,000 applications before I got a job. Don't give up.

They were in a space where they profited big during covid, but they had practically zero profit margin and orders had started to plummet before I even came on. I actually still contract with them to manage their website, but now I'm back to ground zero.

Lesson Learned: That person everyone hates at work is hated for a reason. Even if they're c-suite level. | I started working at a new position awhile back and noticed that someone in our c-suite is particularly disliked at work, let's call him Ron. For a long time I thought people were honestly being mean to Ron, and truthfully, people at all levels from intro to fellow c-suite members are pretty rude and dismissive of Ron, to the point that I've questioned why he still works here. 

A: If  they don't want to listen to him, or take his suggestions seriously then why not hire someone else? 

and, 

B: I honestly don't know how he puts up with it. Everyone low-key shits on him. 

So when I started noticing this, I decided to try to be nice to Ron as best as I can. He's not rude, just exceptionally chatty on pretty boring topics (he's the CFO after all). I found out he had been divorced and just lives at home with some cats, and so I honestly kinda felt for the guy because he clearly doesn't have anyone to talk to. So whenever he had something to say I tried to give him my attention, and I've always followed through on the smile and nod whenever he would yack away and I put genuine effort into trying to make him feel at least somewhat heard at work, because I definitely know what that feels like. Well. Now I know why everyone hates this guy. 

I figured it never hurts to be on the good side of the folks in the c-suite. But apparently all I've accomplished here is being the nail that sticks out for Ron. He now feels the need to provide input and suggestions for my work (literally not a thing to do with his department), and has even complained about my work and delivery. Talking to Ron just put me on his radar as someone he can complain about at work, so yeah, that's the last time I'll make that mistake. My coworkers are petty for sure, and honestly most of them are pretty mean spirited about Ron, but I guess I know now why literally no one likes Ron and gives him constant passive aggressive BS. Lesson learned ya'll. Now I've got this c-suite level guy whose been there since dirt breathing down my neck on stuff, and I was already thinking about leaving/applying because my role is exceptionally underpaid, (if I didn't have assets from selling my business I'd qualify for affordable housing), but goddamn it's brutal job hunting out there.

But yeah, if there's someone at work that everyone hates, even if your workplace is kind of petty, there's still a reason for that.

CS & IT hiring going 'back' to 2019 levels is much worse than it sounds | Most data points to job openings and hiring returning to around 2019 levels. What people seem to conveniently leave out, however, is the immense number of people now competing for these roles compared to then. We have five more years of computer science and IT graduates (about 70,000 to 120,000 per year, with a 40% rise since 2019), five more years of H1B’s, bootcamp graduates, and the self-taught crowd. On top of that, most companies are prioritizing efficiency, meaning juniors aren’t being hired—it’s primarily seniors or higher-level positions being filled, and even they are struggling.

Right now, a fair comparison is the early 2000s dot-com bust, due to the ratio of total people in the field to available job openings. Really this market is affecting nearly all white-collar comfy office jobs, but tech has been hit the hardest due to the perceived lower barrier to entry and higher pay.

A startup wanted me to work 10 am to 10 pm, 6 days a week | So some time ago [I was laid off from a startup](https://www.reddit.com/r/cscareerquestions/comments/171l6nv/just_got_laid_off_fired_from_a_startup_after_6/) and I started looking for work. In my emails was this recruiter from an NYC company called Fiber.ai, they do some AI bullshit, same as every other YC company that came out in the last couple of years. After talking to their recruiter for a sad moment, I wanted to share the company's (now deleted, or sadly, filled) [job post](https://web.archive.org/web/20230930112831/https://www.ycombinator.com/companies/fiber-ai/jobs/EqV3p4a-full-stack-engineer), and it has some incredible gems:

- > $80K - $160K / 0.10% - 0.50%

    - Ah, starting off strong, who wouldn't want to make $80k in NYC? I won't even bother mentioning the pitiful equity.

- > We raised >$4M, generate >$25k MRR, and signed 3 paid pilots with mid-market enterprises that will cumulatively convert at $470,000. We achieved all of this in just 6 weeks and are looking for someone who's *self-driven and autonomous* to build product that customers will be using every day as a core part of their workflows.

    - Translation: you will be doing all of the work.

- > This job will require you to be a Swiss army knife of an engineer

    - Translation: *you will be doing all of the work*. Better than a rockstar, I suppose. Their list of tasks is also hilarious, they're looking for 5 different engineers, not one.

- > We’re looking for a talented full-stack engineer to help us build out our automated prospecting, enrichment, and email personalization pipeline alongside our CTO, Neel, who was formerly at Google and did CS at Harvard (top 5%). You’ll be wrangling tens of gigabytes of data a day, using GPT4 and genAI tools to create hyper-personalized messaging at scale

    - Hear that? *Tens* of gigabytes! Nevermind the authority-building through working at *Google* and going to *Harvard*, what prestige!

- > We will be working 3 days in person in NYC 10am to 10pm in a private office with flexibility to work 2 days remote. We are looking for someone self-driven who can work 55-60 hours a week, and we will compensate you well to make it worth your while.

    - And here's the best part, work 10 am to 10 pm daily (the recruiter specifically said it's a 6 day workweek from Monday through Saturday), and they'll make it "worth my while" with $80k. Classic. 

---

This is not even to talk about their interview process which was just abysmal. The recruiter calls me and tells me about the work schedule, and I just couldn't believe it at first. Do they really expect people to work 12 hour days? He said, yeah, but only for 18 months until the product is off the ground, as if that's any better. Just out of sheer curiosity, I asked what the next step was.

He said you'll have to do a take-home project. Here we go again, I thought, another take home, another rejection. I had previously done one a week prior which they explicitly told us was to be done in no more than 2 hours. It was a basic React/Node/TypeScript task, creating an API and creating a frontend to display it. So I thought it'd be in the same line, but I should have known from the 60 hours a week part that they'd want something completely insane.

It was a *20 hour* take-home project. That is literally half of a normal working week. I was so flabbergasted that it was literally *10x* the length of my previous take-home that I asked the recruiter, exactly how many people have actually gone through the take-home? Sheepishly, he said none, and I said, wow, what a surprise, who'd have thought? After that, I told the recruiter to tell the founders that I'm pretty sure only people with no life would want to join their company (which is likely exactly the kind of person they're looking for), and then I hung up the phone.

Good riddance, and may God have mercy on the soul of whomever they filled that position with. And that layoff I was a part of? I'm doing a lot better now at a larger company, getting paid more and doing less work. Startups are something else, man.

What happened to the industry to cause such a shift in hiring and layoffs? | I’m really terrible at Reddit formatting, so this will probably seem like a blob of text. 

So many people are incorrectly saying that AI is the driving reason for the mass layoffs, non-hiring, and the downward trend of anything software development related. 

AI is a _contributing_ factor to the *difficulty* of getting hired at entry level positions at companies, but that’s a standard bar push. 

But what’s *truly* influencing the mass layoffs, hiring freezes, and shrinking investment into developing proprietary and innovative technologies in America isn’t AI. 

It’s a tax credit rewrite that was never supposed to take effect. 

Law and legislation is boring, but this piece specifically, is important for all of you. It impacts your life, your industry, how you’re paid, what the Chief Financial Officer sees and uses to justify paying you six figures, and your tax rebates if you’re planning to start or work in a startup.

I’m going to lay out the facts in a (hopefully) objective way. 

The credit I’m talking about:

The Research and Development Tax Credit under IRC Tax Code 174. 

The law that changed it:
The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017 (specifically under section 13206). 

This provision was initially drafted by Kevin Brady (R-TX), and advocated significantly for by Republican lawmakers. 

The House of Representatives vote:
227 Republicans For
13 Republicans Against

0 Democrats For
192 Democrats Against

The Senate vote:
51 Republicans For
0 Republicans Against

0 Democrats For
47 Democrats, and 2 Independents Against 

The final result:
Signed into law by President Donald Trump on December 22, 2017. 

Date it took effect:
January 1, 2022

Why so late?
A fun, gimmicky workaround to the Byrd Rule and to delay costly tax hikes until after the 5-year mark, while cashing in on any revenue after the 10-year mark. 

In short, it was a play to look fiscally responsible, but didn’t provide any tax cuts. It just kicked the can down the road and offset immediate tech conglomerate backlash. 

They assumed that this provision would be removed or indefinitely delayed by future Congress, but they didn’t. 

Previous:
Prior to 2022, businesses were able to immediately (same year tax break) cash in and deduct R&D expenses, including software developer and other IT professionals’ salaries, IT infrastructure changes, engineer innovation in all sectors, and more. 

After 2022:
All of the expenses covered by the R&D credit now has to be capitalized and amortized. 

For domestic research, they are required to amortize over 5 years. 

For foreign research, they are required to amortize over 15 years. 

Meaning that, prior to 2022, a $1M investment into software development and cyber security would be fully deductible for fiscal year 22. 

Now, that same $1M investment into those same fields would only allow for $200k to be deductible for the fiscal year, and the remaining $800k would need to be spread out over the remaining four.

Which resulted in layoffs, frozen hiring, cash flow strain for startups and tech firms, and immediate tax burden on companies employing R&D-based that persists to today. 

BUT! There is a bipartisan bill that’s going through Congress right now to reverse it and retroactively apply the lost tax credits back to businesses from 2021 forward, but we’ll see where it goes!

The new H1B visa 100k fee will be good for US software engineers seeking jobs | As a VP and hiring manager at a software company, I see roughly 40 U.S. citizens or green card holders apply for every 200 applicants, meaning around 160 are on H1B visas.

Because of their strong desire to stay in America, many H1B applicants are willing to accept much lower salaries, sometimes as low as 60% of what a U.S. citizen or green card holder would earn, just to get hired and remain in the country. This allows them to outcompete American candidates by driving wages down.

Once hired, they become locked into the company. If they ever ask for a raise, we hold all the leverage because letting them go could force them to leave the country. This dynamic creates an unfair system where companies effectively control these workers and harms fair competition.

Over the past decade, this trend has only worsened, making it harder for U.S. citizens and green card holders to compete for jobs. 

This taken action might restore the hiring landscape in favor of U.S. citizens and green card holders.

If you're a felon and wonder why you won't get hired | I have learned once again to not self-incriminate on applications, etc. It is certainly not the business of the recruiter. The recruiter doesn't know the policy on background checks. It is best to answer "no" on applications when asked about felonies (it is unlawful in some states to ask this on applications). It is between you and the background check company. At that point, they will pass on their findings to the HR department. It is in black and white for the appropriate department to evaluate instead of an individual person who isn't trained in this area.

I went ahead and accepted 3 job offers to start around the same time. In order to narrow it down, I told the recruiter for AWS that I had felonies (I told him I had a theft and a drug possession). He pulled the offer from me. In the meantime, the background check cleared from my first job offer.

Right out of the bootcamp I was lucky enough to land an internship at a small government organization via a connection through the bootcamp.

I was then converted to full time after the internship. It was a pretty modest junior web developer role that only paid about $50k/yr but the work environment and mentorship were phenomenal. And at least it had some pension and healthcare benefits. Over the last 22 months I've learned a ton and have been given the freedom to make some large and meaningful contributions to the company's flagship products.

Sadly we've been hit with some pretty heft budget cutbacks. As the most junior person at the company I'm first on the chopping block and was laid off. As I was being let go, the organization's director offered me a Teaching Assist position at the bootcamp I had graduated. Apparently part of their deal with the bootcamp requires they contribute a certain amount of developer hours towards instruction.

She wanted to try something new with a single dev doing the Teaching Assistant thing full time (previously they'd have the more senior devs rotate in every now and then). Currently I work 3 days from home and 2 days in-office. The teaching role would be 4 days a week on-site. I would still be employed by my current organization and keep the title of "Junior Web Developer". My compensation would be cut down to $30k/yr AND no benefits.

I have a few days to think it over but am tempted to pass on the "opportunity". Would that be a mistake? It would be nice to add a third year as a junior developer to my resume along with some teaching experience but I feel like they're really just taking advantage of me.

Alternatively I can try to test the waters in this absolutely awful job market. But don't foresee myself getting anywhere. I know tons of people with CS degrees that are having no luck whatsoever. I could also alternatively collect unemployment while working towards an accelerated degree program myself and hope things get better in a year or two.

I feel stuck between two kinda terrible options. Any advice is very much appreciated. Thanks!

Found out the other finalist who got hired is crashing and burning | This is a rant. I don't really know who to vent to right now so apologies if this isn't supposed to be on this subreddit.

About 5 months ago, I was one of two finalists for a senior manager role at a nonprofit organization. Unfortunately, after a 4 hour final interview with executive director and other members of the leadership team, I didn't get the role... It was a painful and demoralizing experience.

Turns out, from what people who currently work there have told me, this person is not only struggling but essentially going down in flames. They're making basic mistakes, committing faux pas in meetings with senior leadership, the board, and outside policy makers, burning bridges with other department staff, and haven't pushed one single project to the finish line...

I know I shouldn't be happy about hearing someone fail so miserably at their job in the current job environment but it's especially frustrating to learn this while knowing that I was highly qualified for the role.

Someone I know who just got a leadership role there told me that I was the preferred candidate from the team. But apparently office politics played a large part in the other finalist getting hired. There were two hiring managers and one saw me as a threat to their position. They pushed hard for the other candidate... I saw their LinkedIn profile, and while having strong technical skills, the vast majority of their background has been in the private sector... Aside from some volunteering, they literally have no professional background in this field and zero legislative campaign experience which were important skills or experiences needed for this role... In comparison, I have 9 years of experience in this sector and several state legislative wins...

What I learned about hiring practices is that, the most qualified candidate, even with inside connections, won't get the job. It's entirely out of your hands...

It's all so frustrating...
